title: Types of Noise
concept: Noise doesn't always look the same. Gaussian noise is a gentle, random static spread across every pixel, while salt-and-pepper noise flips scattered individual pixels to pure black or pure white.

options.text: Random, unwanted variation in pixel values that isn't part of the real scene
A deliberate filter effect applied by the photographer
The sharpest, most in-focus part of a photo

exercises.explain: Right — noise is random, unwanted variation. Today we'll see that this randomness comes in more than one flavor.

cards.title: Not all grainy photos look alike
cards.body: Compare a photo from an old low-light sensor to one hit with corrupted transmission errors, and they look nothing alike. One is a soft, all-over shimmer; the other has scattered bright and dark specks like tiny scratches. Noise comes in different shapes.

cards.title: Gaussian noise vs. salt-and-pepper noise
cards.body: Gaussian noise nudges every pixel's value up or down by a small random amount, like a gentle all-over static. Salt-and-pepper noise instead corrupts a scattered handful of individual pixels completely, flipping them to pure white ("salt") or pure black ("pepper") while leaving the rest untouched.

cards.title: TV static vs. spilled salt and pepper
cards.body: Gaussian noise is like the fuzzy hiss on an old TV screen — every pixel wobbles a little, all the time. Salt-and-pepper noise is like someone actually flicking grains of salt and pepper onto the photo — a few scattered pixels turn stark white or black, while the rest of the picture is untouched.

cards.title: Where each type comes from
cards.body: A dim room with a grainy camera sensor typically produces Gaussian noise — a soft shimmer everywhere. A glitchy camera connection or a faulty memory chip is more likely to produce salt-and-pepper noise — isolated pixels snapping to full black or full white.

cards.title: One fix doesn't cure both
cards.body: It's tempting to think "noise is noise" and any denoising trick works on all of it. It doesn't — because the two types behave so differently, the best tool for cleaning up Gaussian noise isn't the best tool for salt-and-pepper noise, as you'll see in the next two lessons.

cards.title: Gaussian vs. salt-and-pepper
cards.body: How each type disturbs the pixel grid:

left.text:
Every pixel shifts slightly
Soft, all-over shimmer

right.text:
A few scattered pixels flip fully
Stark black or white specks

exercises.prompt: What does Gaussian noise do to an image?

options.text: Nudges every pixel's value up or down by a small random amount
Flips a scattered handful of pixels to pure black or white
Deletes random pixels entirely, leaving gaps
Only affects pixels along the edges of objects

exercises.explain: Correct — Gaussian noise is a gentle, all-over random shift applied to every pixel.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Salt-and-pepper noise typically shows up as a handful of pixels turning stark black or white, rather than every pixel shifting slightly.
exercises.explain: True. Salt-and-pepper noise corrupts scattered individual pixels completely, unlike Gaussian noise's gentle all-over shift.

exercises.sentence: Salt-and-pepper noise flips scattered pixels to pure black or pure ___.
exercises.explain: White is correct — the "salt" in salt-and-pepper noise refers to pixels flipped to pure white.

exercises.prompt: A photo shows a handful of isolated pixels randomly snapped to full black or full white, while the rest of the image looks normal. Which type of noise is this?

options.text: No noise at all — this is normal detail

exercises.explain: Isolated pixels snapping fully to black or white, rather than a gentle shimmer across the whole image, is the signature of salt-and-pepper noise.

exercises.prompt: You're given two noisy photos: Photo A has a soft, even shimmer across every pixel. Photo B has scattered pixels that are pure black or pure white against an otherwise clean background. What type of noise does each have?

options.text: Photo A has Gaussian noise; Photo B has salt-and-pepper noise
Both photos have the exact same type of noise, just at different strengths
Photo A has salt-and-pepper noise; Photo B has Gaussian noise
Neither photo has real noise — both effects are added on purpose in editing

exercises.explain: Exactly — an even shimmer across every pixel is Gaussian noise, while scattered pure-black/white pixels are salt-and-pepper noise. Next: a filter built specifically to fight one of these types.
